title: Causal Inference in Platform Markets: A Difference-in-Differences Approach to Pricing Algorithm Effects
description: A 55-page empirical economics paper using DID design to study algorithmic pricing

findings.text: The parallel trends assumption is tested only visually (Figure 2) without formal statistical tests. Pre-treatment event study coefficients should be reported with confidence intervals to validate the DID design.

The treatment variable (algorithm adoption) may be endogenous — platforms that adopt pricing algorithms likely differ systematically from non-adopters. An instrumental variable approach or matching estimator should supplement the DID.
Standard errors are clustered at the platform level (N=47), which may be too few clusters for reliable inference. Authors should report wild cluster bootstrap p-values as a robustness check.

The sample period (2019-2022) includes COVID-19, which caused massive demand shocks in platform markets. The paper does not adequately address how pandemic effects interact with algorithm adoption timing.
Table 4 reports heterogeneous treatment effects by market concentration but does not account for multiple hypothesis testing. Bonferroni or FDR corrections should be applied.
The welfare analysis in Section 7.2 relies on a linear demand model that contradicts the non-linear pricing patterns shown in Figure 5. This internal inconsistency undermines the welfare conclusions.

Key papers on algorithmic collusion (Calvano et al. 2020, Assad et al. 2024) are cited but their findings are mischaracterized — both find evidence of supra-competitive pricing, not the 'ambiguous' effects claimed in the literature review.
The data section mentions that 12% of observations are dropped due to 'data quality issues' but provides no details on which observations or what criteria were used. This threatens external validity.
Robustness check using propensity score matching (Table A3) shows treatment effects that are 40% smaller than the main specification but this discrepancy is not discussed in the text.
